name: Type-and-Effect Systems
summary: Extends a type to describe both the result of an expression and the side effects, capabilities, or control transfers it may perform. Effects may represent I/O, exceptions, allocation, mutation, async suspension, nondeterminism, region access, capabilities, or user-defined algebraic operations. Algebraic effects and handlers generalize exceptions; row-shaped or capability-shaped representations track which effects are in scope.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
A type-and-effect system gives expressions both a result type and an effect description. Effects may represent I/O, exceptions, allocation, mutation, async suspension, nondeterminism, region access, capabilities, or user-defined algebraic operations.

The distinguishing axis across designs is *what shape effects take in the type*: a fixed set of marker classes (Java checked exceptions, Rust `?` boundaries), a row of named operations (Koka, OCaml polymorphic variants over effects), a capability set (Effekt, Pony, Wyvern), or implicit propagation by structural typing rules (Frank's bidirectional discipline). Each shape has predictable consequences for inference, error messages, and runtime cost.

Runtime scheduling and execution-strategy concerns belong in concurrency records; effect handlers realised as a concurrency substrate (delimited continuations, fibers, scheduler integration) are owned by `data/effect-handler-lowering` and `data/virtual-threads#ocaml-effect-handlers`. This record focuses on type rules.

sections.title: What effect systems can support
Algebraic effects and handlers
Effect rows and capability sets
